variables.v_AB: Velocity of A relative to B = v_A − v_B (m/s)
variables.v_m: Velocity of man in still water (m/s)

locked_facts: Velocity of A relative to B: v_AB = v_A − v_B (vector subtraction)
Velocity of B relative to A: v_BA = v_B − v_A = −v_AB
River crossing — minimum time: swim perpendicular to bank, t_min = d/v_m, drift = v_r × d/v_m
River crossing — minimum drift (zero drift): swim at angle θ upstream where sin(θ) = v_r/v_m (only if v_m > v_r)
Rain-man problem: v_rain,man = v_rain − v_man; tilt umbrella at angle tan⁻¹(v_man/v_rain) from vertical
Relative acceleration: a_AB = a_A − a_B; if both have same acceleration, relative motion is uniform
truth_statements: Relative velocity is obtained by vector subtraction: v_AB = v_A − v_B
For minimum time of river crossing, the swimmer must swim perpendicular to the river bank regardless of current
For zero drift in river crossing, the swimmer must have speed greater than the river current (v_m > v_r)
In the rain-man problem, the rain appears to come from a direction determined by relative velocity
When two objects have the same acceleration, their relative velocity remains constant (relative acceleration is zero)

aha_moment.what_to_show: Two river-crossing strategies side by side: (1) swimmer going straight across reaches the other bank first but drifts downstream, (2) swimmer angling upstream takes longer but lands directly opposite. Time bars and drift bars compared.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Time bar shorter for perpendicular crossing, drift bar zero for angled crossing — you cannot minimize both simultaneously
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think swimming at an angle against the current is always better. The simulation shows it minimizes drift but increases crossing time.

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: Two objects A and B moving with velocity vectors shown. The relative velocity v_AB = v_A − v_B is constructed graphically using the triangle law of vector subtraction.
simulation_states.emphasis: Relative velocity is a vector subtraction — show the geometric construction clearly

simulation_states.label: River Problem
simulation_states.description: A river with visible current flowing left to right. Swimmer starts from one bank. First attempt: swims straight across (perpendicular). Path curves downstream due to current. Reaches other bank quickly but with drift.
simulation_states.emphasis: Perpendicular swimming gives minimum time but causes drift. Resultant velocity is the vector sum of swimmer velocity and river current.

simulation_states.label: Aha — Two Strategies
simulation_states.description: Both strategies shown simultaneously: (1) perpendicular path with drift marked, (2) angled-upstream path landing directly opposite. Time comparison and drift comparison shown as bar charts beside the river.
simulation_states.emphasis: Minimum time path has drift; zero drift path takes longer. You cannot have both. sin(θ) = v_r/v_m for zero drift.

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive mode: adjust swimmer speed and river current speed. See how drift and crossing time change. A rain-man mode toggle shows rain falling with man walking — umbrella angle updates in real time.
simulation_states.emphasis: When v_m < v_r, zero drift is impossible — experiment with the slider to discover this limit. In rain mode, walk faster to see umbrella tilt increase.

common_misconceptions.belief: To cross the river in minimum time, you should swim at an angle against the current
trigger_phrases: angle se swim karo toh jaldi pahunchoge
current ke against toh time kam hoga
common_misconceptions.correction: NCERT shows that minimum crossing time is achieved by swimming perpendicular to the bank. Swimming upstream increases time. The perpendicular strategy minimizes time; the upstream-angled strategy minimizes drift.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show two swimmers starting together — perpendicular one reaches the other bank first despite drifting

common_misconceptions.belief: Relative velocity is just the difference of speeds (scalar subtraction)
common_misconceptions.correction: Relative velocity is a VECTOR subtraction: v_AB = v_A − v_B. Direction matters. Two cars at 60 km/h going in opposite directions have relative speed 120 km/h, but same direction gives 0.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show same-speed objects with different relative velocities depending on angle between their velocity vectors

legend.meaning: Velocity of A relative to B = v_A − v_B
Speed of swimmer in still water
Angle of swimming direction from bank normal
Downstream displacement when reaching opposite bank
